title: Shepherd & Flock
description: The image of God as shepherd caring for his people. From Abel the first shepherd through David the shepherd-king to Christ the Good Shepherd, this metaphor captures divine provision, protection, and intimate knowledge of each sheep.

stops.label: God Who Has Shepherded Me
stops.development: Jacob blesses Joseph's sons, naming God as 'the God who has been my shepherd all my life long to this day.'
stops.what_changes: The shepherd metaphor is applied to God for the first time. Divine care is described in terms of the most familiar occupation in the ancient world.
stops.bridge_to_next: Jacob named God as his shepherd from the perspective of a long life's retrospect. His descendant David, who actually tended sheep before tending a nation, transformed that personal testimony into Israel's most beloved expression of trust.

stops.label: The LORD Is My Shepherd
stops.development: David's psalm of complete trust: green pastures, still waters, the valley of the shadow of death, the overflowing cup, goodness and mercy.
stops.what_changes: The shepherd image becomes Israel's defining expression of divine care. Every element of pastoral provision is theologized.
stops.bridge_to_next: Psalm 23 celebrated God as the shepherd of the individual soul. But David himself was also a shepherd — not just of sheep but of God's people. The historical psalms connected David's pastoral background to his royal vocation.

stops.label: David Shepherds Israel
stops.development: God chose David from the sheepfolds to shepherd his people. 'With upright heart he shepherded them and guided them with his skillful hand.'
stops.what_changes: Human kingship is described as shepherding. The good king mirrors the divine shepherd's care for the flock.
stops.bridge_to_next: David shepherded Israel with skill and integrity, but the kings who followed him did not. By Ezekiel's day, Israel's leaders had so thoroughly failed as shepherds that God pronounced devastating judgment on them.

stops.label: Woe to the False Shepherds
stops.development: Israel's leaders are condemned as shepherds who feed themselves instead of the flock. The sheep are scattered and devoured.
stops.what_changes: The shepherd metaphor becomes a standard of judgment for leaders. Failed shepherding is covenant-breaking with the most vulnerable.
stops.bridge_to_next: Ezekiel condemned the false shepherds who fed themselves and neglected the flock. But the oracle did not end in condemnation — it pivoted to an astonishing promise: God himself would step in to do what human leaders had refused to do.

stops.label: God Will Shepherd His Flock
stops.development: God himself declares: 'I myself will search for my sheep... I will rescue them... I will feed them with good pasture.'
stops.what_changes: God replaces failed human shepherds with himself. The divine shepherd will do what human leaders refused to do.
stops.bridge_to_next: God promised to shepherd his scattered flock personally, seeking the lost and binding the injured. Centuries later, Jesus claimed that role with deliberate precision, identifying himself as the good shepherd and adding a dimension Ezekiel had not imagined: the shepherd laying down his life.

stops.label: The Good Shepherd
stops.development: Jesus declares: 'I am the good shepherd. The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep.' He knows his sheep by name.
stops.what_changes: Ezekiel 34's promise of God shepherding his flock is fulfilled christologically. The good shepherd is simultaneously divine and self-sacrificing.
stops.bridge_to_next: Jesus the good shepherd laid down his life for the sheep, combining Ezekiel's promised divine shepherd with Isaiah's suffering servant. After the resurrection and ascension, Peter described the ongoing relationship between the risen Christ and his people in the same pastoral language.

stops.label: Shepherd and Overseer of Souls
stops.development: Believers who were 'straying like sheep have now returned to the Shepherd and Overseer of your souls.'
stops.what_changes: The shepherd image extends to spiritual oversight. Christ shepherds not merely bodies and communities but souls.
stops.bridge_to_next: Peter called Christ the "Shepherd and Overseer of your souls," extending the metaphor from earthly care to spiritual guardianship. The book of Revelation completes the image with a paradox that captures the entire biblical story: the sacrificed Lamb becomes the eternal shepherd.

stops.label: The Lamb Will Shepherd Them
stops.development: The Lamb at the center of the throne 'will be their shepherd, and he will guide them to springs of living water.'
stops.what_changes: The paradox is complete: the Lamb who was sacrificed becomes the eternal shepherd. Vulnerability and authority merge in the final vision.
